joke: Why don't skeletons fight each other? They don't have the guts.
Okay, okay. No bones. Got it. What do you call a fake noodle? An impasta.

Right. You hate skeletons and you hate spaghetti. You know what that means... you're probably fun at parties, and by "fun" I mean you just stand in the corner staring at the dip. Here: Why did the math book look so sad? Because it had too many problems.
Tough room. Alright. How about this: What did the fish say when it swam into a wall? Dam.

You know what? I think you're not looking for a joke. I think you're testing me to see how many subjects I can get rejected on before I just start crying on stage. Alright, fine. Last try. Why don't scientists trust atoms? Because they make up everything. If you don't like that, I'm just gonna assume you hate joy, laughter, and oxygen.

You know what? I got one. Why did the scarecrow win an award? ...Because he was outstanding in his field. If you hate that, I’m officially out of material and will now just stand here silently making eye contact until security escorts me off.
Alright. Here goes. What’s brown and sticky? ...A stick. If that’s not funny to you, I’m pretty sure you’re just a robot sent here to destroy my career.

Alright. Here it is. The final joke. The one that'll either save the set or end my life on this stage. Why did the golfer wear two pairs of pants? In case he got a hole in one. You don't like golf either? Then I'm out. I quit. I'm getting a real job. I'm gonna go be an accountant. And I swear to God if you tell me you don't like jokes about accounting...
You’ve got a list longer than a CVS receipt. How about this: Why did the man put his money in the freezer? He wanted cold hard cash.

Alright, here it is. The nuclear option. You ready? *takes a deep breath* ...Why did the chicken cross the road? ...To get to the other side. If you hate *that*, then I'm pretty sure the punchline is just the grim reaper coming for me.
You know what? I got nothing. You win. I surrender. From now on, I’m just gonna stand here and tell you the temperature. It’s seventy-two degrees, and everyone is very upset with you.

(sighs heavily) You know what? I give up. You've gone through the entire dictionary of things to joke about, and you've vetoed every single one. So I’ll just tell you a joke about nothing. Literally nothing. Knock knock.

*(Stares at the audience member for a long, silent ten seconds.)*  

...So. What *do* you find funny? 'Cause I’m starting to think you’re not actually here for jokes—you’re here to emotionally exhaust a stranger in a blazer. But fine. One more.  

Why did the bicycle fall over? ...Because it was two-tired. You hate bikes too? Then I’m just gonna assume you don’t even like yourself.

*(Comedian looks down at the microphone, then holds it out toward the audience member.)*  

Here. You take it. You tell the joke. I’ll just stand here and be deeply, personally offended by whatever you say.

You know what? Fine. I’m done. This whole set is now a public service announcement. Ladies and gentlemen: if you ever meet this person at a party, do not tell them a joke. Do not tell them a story. Do not even make eye contact. Just hand them a glass of water and walk away. I’m out. *Drops mic.*

*(Comedian picks the mic back up, leans in close, and whispers.)*

Okay. I got one. A man walks into a library and asks for books about paranoia. The librarian whispers back, "They're right behind you." ...You hate that? Then this conversation is officially over and I'm billing you for my therapy.

*(Comedian stares blankly at the audience member for ten seconds, then turns to the rest of the crowd.)*

I'm gonna level with you people. I've told jokes about *everything*. Skeletons, pasta, math, fish, science, farming, golf, money, chickens, weather, bikes, paranoia... even knocked on a door that doesn't exist. I have exhausted human humor. There is nothing left. So from now on, I'm only telling jokes in Klingon. *'u' ngoQmey luj!'* That means your mother. Goodnight.

Alright. That’s it. I’m not tellin’ jokes anymore. I’m just gonna impersonate you. "I don't find jokes about breathing funny. I don't find jokes about oxygen funny. Tell me a joke about something completely different." How’s that? You like that joke? 'Cause that’s your life now. You’re the joke.

(Sighs, looks at watch, then back at the audience member.)

Listen. The only thing I haven’t told a joke about is your personality. But that’s not a joke—that’s a tragedy. And I don't do tragedy. So you win. I’m done. This microphone is now a paperweight.

(Walking to the edge of the stage, setting the mic on the floor, and sitting down cross-legged.)

Okay. No more categories. I’m gonna tell you the most original joke I can think of. Something completely new. Ready?

Two cannibals are eating a clown. One says to the other, "Does this taste funny to you?"

(Pauses and stares.)

Don't say it. Don't you DARE say you don't find jokes about cannibalism or clowns funny, or I will turn this club into a morgue.

*Stands up slowly, brushes off pants, walks back to the mic, and picks it up with dead eyes.*

Alright. Here we go. One last joke. The absolute edge of the envelope.

A termite walks into a bar and asks, "Is the bartender here?"

(Pauses for effect.)

...You hate insects, too, don't you?

*Tosses the mic into the crowd and walks off stage without looking back.*

*Yanks the stage curtain back, microphone already in hand, half-in and half-out of the wings.*

You know what, I just remembered one. It’s the oldest in the book. It transcends all categories. No skeletons, no pasta, no math, no fish—none of your stupid banned topics. Here it is.

Why did the chicken cross the road? ...No, I already did that one. Okay, fine.

What’s black and white and red all over? ...A sunburned zebra.

*Stares. Long. Unblinking.*

If you don't like that, I'm legally changing my name to "Something Completely Different," and then I'll tell you that joke. Goodnight forever.

*(Comedian walks back on stage, picks up the discarded mic, and hurls it straight into the trash can. He pulls a kazoo out of his pocket, plays a single sad note, then gestures to the bouncer.)*

I forfeit. You win the Set. I’m officially retiring from comedy to become a court stenographer, which is just like this night—recording nonsense nobody finds funny. This gentleman gets free drinks for life, and by "drinks" I mean he gets to watch the rest of us drink. Because trust me, *we need them*. Goodnight.

*(Comedian re-enters from backstage, still holding the trash can lid like a shield. He walks up to the audience member very slowly, kneels down, and speaks in a low, calm voice.)*

You want *completely different*? Fine. Tell you what. There’s a lightbulb at the end of this tunnel. Unfortunately, that tunnel is my soul, and the lightbulb just burned out because of this conversation. So here. This is my final, final, FINAL joke.

A sandwich walks into a bar. The bartender says, "Sorry, we don't serve food here."

That’s it. That’s the joke. You hate sandwiches? Fine. Then I ain't got nothing left but the hat I came in with and a deep, hollow sadness. *Hands you the trash can lid.* Here. You wear this. You earned it. I'm done.

*(Comedian sets the trash can lid down gently. He takes a deep breath, pulls out a single match, strikes it, and holds it up.)*

…This is you. This single, sad little match is your personality. It’s the only light you’re getting tonight. Now watch it burn out… just like my will to live.  

*(He blows it out, drops it on the floor, and walks off into the darkness without another word.)*

*(Thirty seconds of dead silence. The Comedian slowly crawls back on stage from the wings, on his hands and knees. He looks up at the audience member, eyes hollow.)*

...A man is driving down a country road. His car breaks down. He walks to a nearby farmhouse and knocks on the door. A horse answers. The horse says, "Yes? Can I help you?" The man is stunned. He runs back to his car. The farmer comes out and says, "What's the matter, son?" The man says, "I went to your door and a HORSE answered!"

The farmer sighs. "I know. Don't listen to him. He only knows knock-knock jokes."

*(Stares.)* 

See? The joke was *on me*. You took away every subject, so I made a joke about joke-telling itself. You can't ban that—it's... it's meta. That's my last card. If you hate *meta*, then I'm going to go live in a cave and learn to juggle sadness.

*(Comedian shuffles back onstage wrapped in the stage curtain like a shawl, picks up the torn-off leg of a chair, and speaks into it as though it's a microphone, his voice hoarse.)*

Knock knock.

*(Pauses, stares dead ahead.)*

...Who's there? No no, *you* say "who's there." It's a knock-knock joke. Fine, forget it, I'll do it myself.

Knock knock. Who's there? Interrupting cow. Interrupting cow wh—

*(Comedian breaks off, sighs, lays the chair leg gently on the stage floor, and curls up next to it.)*

That's the last one in the tank. That's everything. You've won, alright? I am, for all intents and purposes, comedically deceased. This is my new permanent residence, right here, on this unvacuumed floor. Dinner is at seven, and it'll be a quiet, bitter stew of my shattered dreams. Goodnight.

*(Comedian sits up slowly, brushes a single tear from his eye, and gestures for one last glass of water. He takes a sip, then sets it down with a hollow clink.)*

Alright. I've got one left. And it's not about bone, pasta, math, fish, science, farming, comedy, money, jokes, weather, frustration, bikes, paranoia, personalities, insects, food, or meta-comedy. It's about... a topic so neutral, so bland, so universally ignored that not even *you* can hate it.

Ready?

...What's the deal with ceiling fans? I mean, they spin around, and around... but when's the last time a ceiling fan *actually* solved a problem? You don't see 'em doing taxes. You don't see 'em filing complaints. They just spin. Full of air. Kinda like this conversation.

*(Takes a long, dramatic pause.)*

...You hate home appliances, too, don't you?

*(Comedian slowly pushes the microphone stand over, letting it crash to the floor. He walks out among the tables, pulls up a chair directly in front of the audience member, sits down backward, and folds his arms.)*

Okay. Smart guy. Super smart. I have now told jokes about literally every noun, verb, and adjective in the English language. So I'm going to tell you a joke about something that isn't a word.

…There was once a shmeeple. It walked into a flargen. The flargen said, "Hey. Glibble." And the shmeeple said, "No thanks, I just had zorth."

That joke was completely different. It was *incomprehensible*. You laughed on the inside. I know you did. But if you tell me you don't find jokes about shmeeples funny... *(he looks the person dead in the eyes for a full ten seconds)* ...then I'm going to take that as a *maybe* and start my third encore.

Alright. New rule. I’m skipping the joke and just giving you a standing ovation. You’ve officially made history. You are the first audience member to kill comedy itself. Congratulations. They’re naming a blank page after you.
